coredata.dc:title: RNA-responsive fluorescent carbon dots for fast and wash-free nucleolus imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Xiaohui
Sun, Yuanqiang
Yang, Ran
Qu, Lingbo
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: RNA as a carrier of genetic information plays a critical role in various physiological processes. RNA-rich nucleolus is usually employed as an important biomarker for many malignant diseases. Herein, RNA-responsive fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by a simple microwave method. Due to the presence of cationic benzothiazolium groups in the CDs, a “turn-on” fluorescence signal was achieved between CDs and RNA. The CDs exhibit excellent RNA selectivity and a good linear relationship with a detection limit of 0.62 μg/mL. The small particle size, polarity sensitivity and RNA response behavior of CDs realized fast and wash-free nucleolus imaging effectively. Overall, these CDs provide a powerful potential tool for monitoring cell nucleus activity and elucidating RNA dynamics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent probe
Carbon dots
RNA detection
Nucleolus imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The design strategy, characterization and optical properties of the CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM graph and the size distribution histogram of the CDs (inset). Scale bar = 100 nm. (b) FTIR spectrum. (c) XPS curve.

(a) The reaction time between CDs (10 μg/mL) and RNA (200 μg/mL). (b) The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (10 μg/mL) with increasing concentrations of RNA (0–800 μg/mL). (c) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 560 nm and concentrations of RNA. (d) The fluorescence intensity of the CDs (10 μg/mL) in PBS (10 mM pH 7.4) with 200 μM amino acids, 200 μM ROS, 1 mM cations and anions. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs before and after adding interferences (1: CDs; 2: K+; 3: Ca2+; 4: Na+; 5: Mg2+; 6: Al3+; 7: Zn2+; 8: Fe3+; 9: Cu2+; 10: Ni+; 11: Cr3+; 12: Mn2+; 13: F−; 14: Cl−; 15: Br−; 16: I−; 17: SO4 2−; 18: NO3 −; 19: CO3 2−; 20: Thr; 21: Arg; 22: Met; 23: Pro; 24: Gly; 25: Lys; 26: Leu; 27: Try; 28: Iso; 29: Ala; 30: His; 31: Glu; 32: Asp; 33: Val; 34: Ser; 35: Cys; 36: Hcy; 37: GSH; 38: DA; 39: HS−; 40: NH2OH; 41: H2O2; 42: ClO−; 43: NO2 −; 44: O2·−; 45: DNA; 46: RNA). The error bars represent the mean errors for three tests.

Confocal fluorescence images of living HeLa cells incubated with CDs (5 μg/mL) for (a) 0 s; (b) 5 s; (c) 30 s; (d) 60 s; (e) 90 s; (f) 120 s; (g) 150 s; (h) 180 s. (i) The plot of fluorescence intensity and verse incubation time. The excitation and emission wavelength are 488 nm and 500–600 nm, respectively. Scale bar = 30 μm.

Fluorescence images of living cells co-stained with CDs (5 μg/mL) for 5 min and NucRed™ Live 647 (1 drop) for 15 min. (a) HeLa cells. (b) HL-7702 cells. (c) HepG-2 cells. The excitation and emission wavelength of the NucRed™ Live 647 is 638 nm and 650–750 nm, respectively. Scale bar = 30 μm.

(a) Fluorescence images of CDs-incubated HeLa cells in the presence of different endocytosis inhibitors. The excitation and emission wavelength is 488 nm and 500–600 nm, respectively. (b) Cell internalization percentages of CDs in the presence of different endocytosis inhibitors. Scale bar = 30 μm.
The sensing model of the CDs for RNA response and nucleolus imaging.

head.ce:title: RNA-responsive fluorescent carbon dots for fast and wash-free nucleolus imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Green Catalysis Center, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Green Catalysis Center, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: RNA as a carrier of genetic information plays a critical role in various physiological processes. RNA-rich nucleolus is usually employed as an important biomarker for many malignant diseases. Herein, RNA-responsive fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by a simple microwave method. Due to the presence of cationic benzothiazolium groups in the CDs, a “turn-on” fluorescence signal was achieved between CDs and RNA. The CDs exhibit excellent RNA selectivity and a good linear relationship with a detection limit of 0.62 μg/mL. The small particle size, polarity sensitivity and RNA response behavior of CDs realized fast and wash-free nucleolus imaging effectively. Overall, these CDs provide a powerful potential tool for monitoring cell nucleus activity and elucidating RNA dynamics.

ce:section.ce:para: Ribonucleic acid (RNA), as a carrier of genetic information, plays an important role in the transmission of genetic information, gene regulation, protein synthesis, and reaction catalysts [1–4]. RNA includes ribosomal RNA (rRNA), transition RNA (tRNA) and messenger RNA (mRNA). As the highest content of RNA, rRNA mainly presents in the nucleolus with about 82% proportion, while tRNA accounting for 15%, and mRNA only 3–5% [5,6]. The nucleolus, as a key component in the cell nucleus, is the site of rRNA production, transcription, and processing assembly [7–9], which is closely related to cell mitosis, cell proliferation, cell cycle regulation, aging, and stress responses [10–12]. The size and shape changes of the nucleolus are usually used as indicators of some diseases, therefore the nucleolus is employed in real applications as an important biomarker for the diagnosis of malignant diseases [13]. However, there is only one commercial nucleolus probe so far: SYTO RNA select, and it has some disadvantages including high cost, low selectivity, complex staining steps, and notably only staining fixed cells (not living cells) [14], which severely limits its further applications for real-time imaging and drug delivery.

In decades, due to high selectivity, sensitivity, spatial and temporal resolution, fluorescence imaging holds great promise in subcellular organelles targeted imaging, early disease diagnosis, drug delivery and so on [15–19]. Meanwhile, it not only makes some progress in studying the process of malignant transformation of the tumor, but also provides tools for cancer diagnosis [20]. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new classification of nanomaterials [21–23], have been widely used in bio-imaging, light-emitting diode, photocatalysis, and other research fields [24–27]. For example, Yang et al. [28] reported N-doped red emissive CDs for in vivo imaging. Tan et al. [29] reported p-phenylenediamine derived red-emitting CDs by the catalysis of sulfuric acid and their application in bio-imaging. Zhao et al. [26] reported controllable acidophilic CDs for optoelectronic LED and selective imaging of bacteria. Liu et al. [30] reported red emissive CDs that can cross the blood−brain barrier for noninvasive brain tumor imaging. Thus, intrinsic properties such as easy-to-get precursors, facile synthesis, good biocompatibility, robust stability, and low cost have provided CDs great potential for high-quality cellular imaging. However, the investigations of CDs for fluorescent imaging are remaining at a very early stage and there are few CDs for RNA sensing and nucleolus imaging.

Herein in this work, RNA-responsive fluorescent CDs for nucleolus imaging were developed by a simple microwave method (Scheme 1 ). Due to the existence of the cationic group on CDs, they can specifically respond to negatively charged RNA with a very low detection limit. Furthermore, because of the small size and RNA response of CDs, these CDs can realize fast, convenient and wash-free in situ nucleolus imaging, which has great potential in elucidating RNA dynamics and visual screening of RNA anticancer drugs as well as pharmacodynamic evaluation.

2,4-Dihydroxybenzaldehyde, dichloromethane, ethyl acetate, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol, etc. were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NucRed™ Live 647 and Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) were obtained from Thermo Scientific (Pittsburgh, USA) and Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China), respectively. Various endocytosis inhibitors: chlorpromazine (CPZ), amiloride (AMI), genistein, sulfobromophthalein (BSP), and methyl-β-cyclodextrin (MβCD) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (MO, USA). Milli-Q system of Millipore (Massachusetts, USA) was employed to produce 18.2 MΩ·cm ultrapure water for the followed experiments. DNA and RNA were obtained from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). DNase and RNase were purchased from Beyotime (Jiangsu, China).

Discover SP microwave reactor (CEM, USA) was used to prepare the CDs. The morphology of the CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI-Tecnai G2, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were tested on Bruker Tensor 27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) and Thermo Scientific (ESCALab250, USA), respectively. F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and Agilent UV-8453 spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) were used to measure the emission and absorption spectra, respectively. Fluorescence lifetime was recorded by FLS980 (Edinburgh, England). The toxicity of the CDs was recorded by the Multiskan GO Microplate Spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). Cell images were performed by confocal fluorescence microscope (Leica TCS SP8, Germany).

The CDs were synthesized by a simple microwave method. Firstly, 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde (15 mg) and 2,3-dimethylbenzothiazole iodide (10 mg) were placed in 4 mL absolute ethanol. This solution was transferred to a microwave reactor and heated at 130 °C for 1 h. After the reaction was completed and cooled to room temperature, the CDs were purified through silica column chromatography and a mixture solution of dichloromethane and ethanol (5:1, V:V) was used as the eluent. After evaporating the eluent, the obtained solid was stored at 4 °C for later use.

The toxicity of CDs was examined by using the CCK-8 assay. HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well plates in the presence of 100 μL culture medium. After culturing for 24 h, the culture medium was substituted by 100 μL CDs solution with the concentrations ranging from 0 to 15 μg/mL prepared form fresh medium. After 24 h of incubation, CCK-8 was added into each well cultivating 2 h, the optical density of CCK-8 at 450 nm was recorded. Each cytotoxicity experiment was repeated for three times.

Human liver normal cell (HL-7702), human liver cancerous cells (HepG-2), and human cervical cancer cells (HeLa) were used in the cell experiments. The cells were cultured in complete DMEM (100 μg/mL streptomycin-penicillin) supplemented with 10% FBS in 5% CO2 at 37 °C.

For co-stain experiments, HeLa, HepG-2 and HL-7702 cells were treated with CDs (5 μg/mL) for 5 min and commercial nucleus probe (NucRed™ Live 647, 1 drop) for 15 min. After washed with PBS 3 times, the HeLa cells were incubated with DMEM without phenol red.

For DNase and RNase digestion experiments, after fixed with pre-chilled methanol (−20 °C) for 15 min and washed by PBS for 3 times, HeLa cells were treated with RNase (25 μg/mL) and/or DNase (5 U/mL) for 2 h at 37 °C, followed by being incubated with the CDs for 5 min and washed three times with PBS and then imaged in DMEM without phenol red.
For the wash-free experiment, living cells were treated with CDs (5 μg/mL) for 5 min, and then fluorescence images were recorded without washing.

The cellular uptake pathways of CDs were measured by using various endocytosis inhibitors. First, confocal dishes were employed to seed the living HeLa cells and grow at 37 °C for 24 h. In order to prove the energy-dependent of CDs, the cells were incubated with CDs at 4 °C for 2 h and then imaged after washed with PBS. Correspondingly, different energy-dependent endocytosis inhibitors, e.g. CPZ (5 μg/mL, 2 h), AMI (10 μg/mL, 2 h), MβCD (5 μg/mL, 2 h), BSP (250 μM, 10 min) and genistein (50 μg/mL, 2 h) were chosen to investigate the endocytosis pathway of CDs, followed by being washed with PBS for 3 times and then incubated with CDs (5 μg/mL, 5 min). After then, the cells were further washed with PBS for 3 times. The HeLa cells were imaged in DMEM without phenol red and FL intensities were quantified to derive the uptake percentage of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The design strategy, characterization and optical properties of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: One of the unique characters of RNA is the negative charge, thus the positive charged aromatic materials can selectively bind with RNA driven by electrostatic forces. The RNA responsive CDs should contain aromatic positive groups to get RNA binding and consequently intrinsic nucleolus targeting ability. Thus, 2,3-dimethylbenzothiazolium was selected as the RNA targeting group, which has the reactive methyl groups that can effectively connect with the carbonized cores. Very recently, we reported that 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde as an effective precursor to synthesize the carbon cores of CDs [31] with the advantages of cost-effective, and good photo-stability. Therefore, 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde and 2,3-dimethylbenzothiazolium iodide were selected as the precursors of the RNA-responsive CDs. The hemicyanine group was expected to be formed in the CDs as a result of the reaction between the aldehyde group of 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde and the methyl group of 2,3-dimethylbenzothiazolium iodide. The CDs would show weak fluorescence due to the fast rotation of the double bond of hemicyanine in the excited state. After binding with RNA, the rotation would be restricted and result in strong fluorescence, which could serve as the basis for the selective sensing of RNA.

The CDs were synthesized by 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde and 2,3-dimethylbenzothiazolium iodide in ethanol through the microwave irradiation for 1 h. The morphology of CDs was characterized by TEM (Fig. 1a). TEM image showed uniform dispersion with an average diameter of 6.78 ± 0.3 nm. The smaller size is owing to high carbonization. The functional groups on the surface of the CDs were studied by FTIR and XPS spectrum. As shown in Fig. 1b, there are obvious absorption bands corresponding to different groups: OH (3439 cm−1), CH (2926 cm−1), CO (1720 cm−1), CC (1618 cm−1), aromatic hydrogen (1618, 1571, 15,498, 1465 cm−1) and CS/CO (1126 cm−1) stretching vibration. The XPS spectrum was shown in Fig. 1c, which includes four elements: C1s (284.7 eV), O1s (531.9 eV), N1s (400.3 eV), and S2p (163.7 eV). The atomic percentages were calculated to be 44.43%, 18.02%, 32.72%, and 4.831%. The high-resolution XPS spectrum is shown in Fig. S1, C1s has three peaks: 284.7 eV, 285.7 eV, and 288.4 eV, which can be attributed to the CC/CC, CN/CO/CS, and CO/CN bonds. O1s had two peaks: 531.8 eV and 533.2 eV, which correspond to the CO and COC/COH. N1s had three peaks: 399.2 eV, 400.8 eV, and 403.1 eV, which correspond to CN, CN, and cationic N [32], indicating the existence of positive benzothiazolium groups and hemicyanine structure. S2p had two main peaks: 162.8 and 163.7 eV, which correspond to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2. All these results indicated that the CDs were successfully synthesized.

The optical properties of CDs were measured by UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra in PBS buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.4). As shown in Fig. S2a, the absorption and fluorescence emission bands of CDs are located at 505 nm and 560 nm (using the best excitation wavelength of 500 nm). Due to the uniform surface and size distribution [33], CDs show an excitation-independent-fluorescence behavior. When the excitation wavelength increased from 400 nm to 500 nm, there is no significant shift in the maximum emission wavelength (Fig. S2b). The photostability of CDs is very important for practical applications. Thus, xenon lamp (150 W) was used to irradiate the CDs in the PBS buffer solution for 1800 s. Almost no obvious changes in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 560 nm were observed, which indicated the high photostability of the CDs. In addition, these CDs have good water solubility. As shown in Fig. S2d, when different concentrations (2.5–20 μg/mL) of CDs were added to the PBS buffer solution, the UV absorption peaks at 505 nm increases regularly. According to the experimental results, the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs was 2.277 ns (Fig. S2e).

In addition, the optical properties of CDs in various organic solvents were studied. As shown in Fig. S2f, the CDs in various organic solvent exhibit large differences in the position and intensity of the emission peaks, indicating the polarity-sensitive characteristics of CDs. Due to the lower fluorescence intensity in water, these CDs have good potential to be used for wash-free cell imaging [34].

As shown above, the CDs have plenty of positive benzothiazolium groups. Due to the electrostatic attraction interaction between positively charged CDs and negatively charged RNA [35], the CDs were supposed to respond with RNA. Firstly, the assay time between CDs and RNA was studied. As shown in Fig. 2a, when RNA (200 μg/mL) was added into CDs solution, the fluorescence signal of CDs suddenly increases up to a steady value in 10 s. Thus, CDs can react with RNA in a very short time. In order to investigate the response behavior of CDs to different concentrations of RNA, the standard working curve between RNA concentration and fluorescence intensity was established. As shown in Fig. 2b, under the optimal experimental conditions, with the increase of RNA concentration, the fluorescence of CDs at 560 nm enhances regularly. In the presence of 800 μg/mL RNA, 15-fold enhancement of the fluorescence at 560 nm was observed. The CDs exhibited a fine linear relationship to RNA in the concentration range of 0–200 μg/mL (Fig. 2c), corresponding to regression equation as y = 3.721x + 129.6 (R2 = 0.9903). The detection limit (LOD) was determined to be 0.62 μg/mL. The lower LOD indicated their robust ability to detect RNA sensitively.

In order to test the selectivity of CDs to RNA, fluorescence intensity of CDs with some common reactive substances were studied. As shown in Fig. 2d, after adding various biologically active substances including amino acids, active oxygen, anions and cations, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 560 nm has almost no change otherwise nucleic acid (DNA and RNA). These results indicated that the CDs hardly reacted with these substances and can be used for biological imaging without other interference. Although the CDs showed some fluorescence enhancement after treatment with DNA, this phenomenon is similar to the response of commercialized SYTO RNA select probe with RNA and DNA [36]. In addition, the higher fluorescence intensity may attribute to the hydrophobic environment offered by RNA, which has much larger hydrophobic space when compared to the double-strand DNA [37]. Therefore, the results indicate that CDs have a strong response to RNA with the potential for specific detecting and monitoring of RNA-rich nucleolus.

To investigate the application of CDs in living cells, the cell viability of the CDs was determined by employing CCK-8 (Fig. S3). Even if the concentration of the CDs was up to 15 μg/mL, HeLa cells still exhibited higher cell viability. Thus, the CDs have negligible cytotoxicity and are suitable for the followed cell imaging experiments.

Encouraged by the above results, the fast nucleolus imaging capacity of the CDs was examined. As shown in Fig. 3 , after incubated with CDs for 5 s, bright yellow spots are existed in the cytoplasm and nucleolus, indicating that the CDs can penetrate cell membrane and nucleus membrane quickly. With the increase of incubation time, the nucleolus becomes relatively brighter, indicating that the CDs mainly accumulated nucleolus after entering the cell. When the cells were incubated with CDs for 120 s, the fluorescence intensity of cells reached the plateau, which is much faster than the commercial SYTO RNA select. The fast membrane permeability might be benefit from its small size [38,39]. In order to take the experiments better, the incubation time was set for 5 min as the incubation time. Concentration-dependent fluorescence imaging was shown in Fig. S4, after incubated with CDs for 5 min, 5 μg/mL CDs shows bright yellow fluorescence which is similar to 7.5/10 μg/mL. The CDs (2.5 μg/mL) emit relatively weaker fluorescence intensity. Generally, lower concentrations are an advantage for staining organelles, therefore 5 μg/mL was selected as the optimal incubation concentration for subsequent cell experiments.

According to the results of the spectrum experiment, the CDs show weak fluorescence in PBS buffer with polarity-sensitivity, while showing high fluorescence when binding with RNA. This could serve as the basis for washing-free in living cell imaging. As shown in Fig. S5, HeLa cells were incubated with CDs for 5 min and then captured without or with washing, there are no obvious differences between before-wash and after-wash results. Thus, the negligible background fluorescence and the capacity of wash-free hold great advantages for simplifying cell processing.

To investigate the nucleolus imaging capability of CDs, co-stained experiments of CDs with the commercial nucleus probe (NucRed™ Live 647) in living cells was studied. As shown in Fig. 4 , the nucleolus showed bright yellow fluorescence and located at the nucleolus, indicating that the CDs have excellent nucleolus targeting ability in different cells such as HeLa, HepG-2, and HL-7702 cells. Although SYTO RNA selects as the only commercialized probe, the excitation and emission of it well overlap with the CDs. Therefore, SYTO RNA selection does not suit the co-localization experiments, which indicated the importance to develop more RNA probes for nucleolus imaging.

Although most RNA (especially the rRNA) accumulate in the nucleolus, there are some DNA exists in the nucleolus. To investigate the specific RNA targeting capacity of CDs, digestion experiments were conducted by employing ribonuclease (RNase) and deoxyribonuclease (DNase) to hydrolyze RNA and DNA, respectively. As shown in Fig. S6c, compared to the control group (Fig. S6a), due to the removal of RNA content after digestion, there is a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs that occurred in the cytoplasm and nucleolus. Meanwhile, the nucleolus is hardly seen and the specificity of the nucleolus is lost. While there is no obvious change in the DNase group (Fig. S6b). Fig. S6d proved again that the nucleolus-targeting capacity of CDs resulted from the specific response between CDs and RNA, corresponding to the above spectroscopic experiments.

Due to the high cellular uptake efficiency of CDs, confocal fluorescence microscope was used to research various cellular uptake pathways. For most of the nanomaterials, energy-dependent endocytosis is the main pathway to enter into cells. Thus, HeLa cells were pre-treated with low temperature (4 °C), which decreased the generation of the intracellular adenosine triphosphate (ATP) [40–42]. As shown in Fig. 5 , energy-depleted experiments affected the endocytosis of CDs, leading to a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity, revealing that the endocytosis of CDs might be energy-dependent. Generally, there is five energy-dependent endocytosis, including macropinocytosis, clathrin-mediated endocytosis (CME), OATP protein-mediated endocytosis, caveolae-mediated endocytosis (CvME), and lipid raft-mediated endocytosis (LrME). Thus, five endocytosis inhibitors: chlorpromazine (CPZ, an inhibitor of CME), amiloride (AMI, an inhibitor of macropinocytosis), sulfobromophthalein (BSP, a competitive inhibitor of OATP), genistein (inhibitor of CvME), and the methyl-β-cyclodextrin (MβCD, an inhibitor of LrME) were used to further prove the endocytosis pathway of CDs. As shown in Fig. 5, the results show that only the MβCD group significantly reduces the internalization of CDs, while the effects of other inhibitors are negligible, revealing that CDs were endocytosed into cells via lipid raft-mediated pathway.

In summary, 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde and 2,3-dimethylbenzothiazole iodide were used to form carbonized polymer core and RNA target group to synthesize the CDs by a simple microwave method. The CDs with yellow fluorescence show excellent selectivity and a good linear relationship with a detection limit of 0.62 μg/mL to RNA. Thus, the CDs could target RNA-rich nucleolus realizing intracellular RNA imaging. Due to the small size, RNA-responsive and polarity-sensitive performance of the CDs, they can penetrate cell membranes and nucleus membranes within 2 min and be used for wash-free nucleolus imaging with satisfactory results, which demonstrate that these new developed CDs have great potential in medical and biological studies, such as elucidating RNA dynamics, monitoring organelle activity, etc.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Xiaohui Yin: Data curation, Software, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yuanqiang Sun: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation. Ran Yang: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Lingbo Qu: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Zhaohui Li: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21974125, 21708035), the Foundation for University Key Teacher by Henan Province (2017GGJS007), the Key Scientific Research Project in Universities of Henan Province (19A150048) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2017M620302, 2018T110736).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118381.
